content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (buy) a new bag tomorrow.
distractor_rationale: will buys is grammatically incorrect — will is followed by the base form, not a conjugated verb
going to buy omits the required auxiliary be, making the sentence ungrammatical
buys is simple present and cannot express a future plan decided in advance

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Look at that baby! She ___ (cry) any second now.
distractor_rationale: will cry expresses a general prediction without present evidence, but the visible sign 'look at that baby' demands a going to prediction based on evidence
cries is simple present and cannot describe an imminent future event
is crying describes an action already in progress, but the baby has not started crying yet

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: We ___ (not visit) the museum this weekend because it is closed.
distractor_rationale: will not visiting is ungrammatical — will must be followed by a base verb, not a gerund
don't visit is simple present and cannot express a cancelled future plan
are not visit omits the required going to structure and is grammatically incomplete

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna has already packed her suitcase because she ___ (fly) to her sister's city next Monday.
distractor_rationale: will fly suggests a spontaneous decision made at the moment of speaking, but the packed suitcase shows a pre-arranged plan requiring going to
flies is simple present and cannot express a future travel plan
was going to fly is past tense and contradicts the future time marker next Monday

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The glass is right on the edge of the table — it ___ (fall) if nobody moves it.
distractor_rationale: will fall is used for general predictions without visible evidence, but the glass visibly on the edge provides immediate physical evidence requiring going to
falls is simple present and cannot express an imminent future event based on current evidence
is fallen is grammatically incorrect — fall is an intransitive verb and does not form a passive in this context

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My parents ___ (redecorate) the living room next month — they have already chosen the paint colour.
distractor_rationale: will redecorate implies a decision made now at the moment of speaking, but the chosen paint colour proves the plan was made earlier, requiring going to
are redecorating could describe an action already in progress, which contradicts next month as a future time marker
going to redecorate omits the auxiliary are, making the sentence grammatically incomplete

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: David ___ (not eat) meat anymore — he decided to become vegetarian last week.
distractor_rationale: will not eat suggests a spontaneous future decision, but the sentence specifies the decision was made last week, so going to is required for a pre-planned intention
does not eat is simple present and cannot express a future intention starting from a past decision
is not eaten is passive voice and is grammatically and semantically incorrect here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Sara has signed up for an evening course, so she ___ (study) graphic design every Tuesday for the next three months.
distractor_rationale: will study implies a decision made at the moment of speaking, but signing up for a course is clear evidence of a pre-arranged plan requiring going to
studies is simple present and cannot express a scheduled future commitment
has studied is present perfect and refers to completed past experience, not a future plan

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The mechanic has just looked under the car and says the engine ___ (break down) completely unless we replace the part today.
distractor_rationale: will break down expresses a general future prediction without specific present evidence, but the mechanic's physical inspection provides concrete visible evidence requiring going to for an evidence-based prediction
breaks down is simple present and cannot express a future consequence identified through current evidence
is breaking down describes an action currently in progress, whereas the engine has not yet failed — the breakdown is imminent but future

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The project manager confirmed in this morning's meeting that the team ___ (launch) the new product in early spring, once all the tests are complete.
distractor_rationale: will launch suggests a spontaneous announcement rather than a confirmed pre-arranged plan; the phrase confirmed in this morning's meeting establishes that the decision was already made, requiring going to
launched is past simple and cannot refer to a future event described with once all the tests are complete
is launching could imply the launch is already underway, which contradicts once all the tests are complete as a future condition

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Tom ___ (visit) his grandparents this weekend.
distractor_rationale: will requires the base form without -s; this form is grammatically impossible
the auxiliary be is required before going to; omitting it produces an ungrammatical structure
present simple cannot express a future plan already decided for this weekend

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Look at that little boy on the bike — he ___ (fall) off!
distractor_rationale: will expresses a general prediction without present evidence; here the visible scene provides clear evidence, requiring going to
present simple cannot express an imminent future event predicted from current visual evidence
present continuous describes an action already in progress, but the boy has not yet fallen

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Anna and her sister ___ (not/fly) to the conference next week because they booked train tickets yesterday.
distractor_rationale: will requires the base form; attaching -ing to the main verb produces an ungrammatical construction
do is the auxiliary for present simple negation, not for going to constructions; be is required
to is obligatory between going and the base verb; omitting it makes the structure ungrammatical

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: James has already bought all the ingredients, so he ___ (cook) dinner for the whole family tonight.
distractor_rationale: will is used for spontaneous decisions made at the moment of speaking; buying ingredients beforehand shows a pre-planned intention, which requires going to
the base form of the main verb must follow going to; adding -ing produces an ungrammatical structure
present simple cannot express a future plan; it describes habits or scheduled timetable events only

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The manager has already confirmed the schedule, so the team ___ (start) the new project on Monday morning, not next month as some people expected.
distractor_rationale: will is used for decisions made at the moment of speaking or general predictions without evidence; the confirmed schedule indicates a pre-arranged plan, which requires going to
the auxiliary be must agree with the subject the team (singular collective); omitting is produces an ungrammatical structure
the main verb must remain in base form after going to; adding -ing is ungrammatical regardless of the auxiliary

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom ___ a new bike next week.
distractor_rationale: will + -ing is not a valid verb form in English; will must be followed by a bare infinitive
omits the required auxiliary 'is' before going to, a common learner error
present simple cannot express a future plan or intention

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Look at that glass on the edge of the table! It ___ fall.
distractor_rationale: will is used for general predictions without present evidence; here the visible position of the glass provides clear physical evidence, requiring going to
omits the required auxiliary 'is' before going to, making the verb phrase grammatically incomplete
omits 'to' from the going to structure, making the verb phrase incomplete and ungrammatical

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: We ___ visit our grandparents this Sunday — we already told them.
distractor_rationale: will is used for unplanned decisions made at the moment of speaking; here the plan was already made in advance, requiring going to
omits 'to' after going, making the structure incomplete and ungrammatical
were going to describes a past intention that was not carried out; the plan is still current and for the future

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria has already booked her flights, so she ___ spend her holiday in Italy this summer.
distractor_rationale: will is inappropriate here because the evidence of a pre-existing decision (booked flights) signals a prior plan, which requires going to
omits 'to' from the going to structure, making the verb phrase grammatically incomplete
was going to refers to a past unfulfilled intention; the sentence describes a current confirmed future plan

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The children ___ not ___ play in the garden today because it is raining heavily.
distractor_rationale: will cannot be combined with going to; the negative form requires 'are not going to'
omits 'to' after going, making the structure incomplete and ungrammatical
'is' does not agree with the plural subject 'the children'; the correct auxiliary is 'are'

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: James has saved enough money, and he ___ buy a new laptop before his course starts.
distractor_rationale: will suggests a spontaneous decision made now; the sentence shows James has already saved and planned, so going to is required
omits the auxiliary 'is', making the structure ungrammatical without a finite verb
omits 'to' after going, leaving the infinitive 'buy' without the required connector

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Look at the sky — those black clouds mean it ___ storm very soon.
distractor_rationale: will is used for general predictions with no visible evidence; the visible black clouds provide present evidence, so going to is required
omits 'to' from the structure, making the verb phrase 'is going storm' ungrammatical
was going to indicates a past unfulfilled intention; the prediction is about an imminent future event based on current evidence

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: After months of planning, Anna and her team ___ launch the new product at the trade fair next month.
distractor_rationale: will implies a spontaneous decision; months of prior planning signals a fixed intention, which requires going to
omits 'to' after going, making the infinitive 'launch' grammatically unattached
have gone to is present perfect and describes a completed action of going somewhere, not a future plan

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The doctor says that if David doesn't rest this week, his cold ___ get much worse by the weekend.
distractor_rationale: will is possible here for a general prediction, but the doctor's present clinical assessment provides specific present evidence pointing to an imminent outcome, making going to the more precise and contextually required choice
omits 'to' from the structure, leaving 'is going get' which is ungrammatical
goes to is present simple and cannot express a future prediction; 'to' here wrongly implies movement toward a destination

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Sara told her manager last week that she ___ not ___ accept the new position because it requires too much travel.
distractor_rationale: will cannot be combined with going to in the same verb phrase; the negative of going to is formed with 'is not going to'
'are' does not agree with the singular subject 'she'; the correct auxiliary is 'is'
omits 'to' after going, making 'is not going accept' ungrammatical as the infinitive requires the connector 'to'

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: She going to visit her grandmother this weekend.
content.error_sentence: She going to visit her grandmother this weekend.
content.corrected_sentence: She is going to visit her grandmother this weekend.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: They going to buy a new sofa for the living room.
content.error_sentence: They going to buy a new sofa for the living room.
content.corrected_sentence: They are going to buy a new sofa for the living room.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I will meet my friends at the café tonight — we already booked a table.
content.error_sentence: I will meet my friends at the café tonight — we already booked a table.
content.corrected_sentence: I am going to meet my friends at the café tonight — we already booked a table.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds — it will rain very soon.
content.error_sentence: Look at those dark clouds — it will rain very soon.
content.corrected_sentence: Look at those dark clouds — it is going to rain very soon.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Mark and his brother gonna paint the kitchen walls next Saturday.
content.error_sentence: Mark and his brother gonna paint the kitchen walls next Saturday.
content.corrected_sentence: Mark and his brother are going to paint the kitchen walls next Saturday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The manager will present the new project plan at tomorrow's meeting — she has prepared everything already.
content.error_sentence: The manager will present the new project plan at tomorrow's meeting — she has prepared everything already.
content.corrected_sentence: The manager is going to present the new project plan at tomorrow's meeting — she has prepared everything already.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Anna isn't going to finishes her assignment before the deadline because she started it too late.
content.error_sentence: Anna isn't going to finishes her assignment before the deadline because she started it too late.
content.corrected_sentence: Anna isn't going to finish her assignment before the deadline because she started it too late.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Be careful — that shelf is loaded with heavy boxes and it will fall if you touch it.
content.error_sentence: Be careful — that shelf is loaded with heavy boxes and it will fall if you touch it.
content.corrected_sentence: Be careful — that shelf is loaded with heavy boxes and it is going to fall if you touch it.
